volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: The Two Menaechmuses (Section 4.2.631c - Section 5.1.701)

sections.label: Section 4.2.631c
sections.original: Nihil hoc homine audacius .
sections.translation: Nothing is there more audacious than this fellow . Did I not just now see you standing here before the house , with a chaplet of flowers on , when you were declaring that my headpiece wasn ’ t sound , and declaring that you didn ’ t know me , and saying that you were a foreigner ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.635
sections.original: Quin ut dudum divorti abs te , redeo nunc demum domum .
sections.translation: On the contrary , as some time since I parted with you , so I ’ m now returning home at last .

sections.label: Section 4.2.636
sections.original: Novi ego te . non mihi censebas esse , qui te ulciscerer .
sections.translation: I understand you . You didn ’ t think it was in my power to take vengeance upon you ; i ’ faith , I ’ ve told it all to your wife .

sections.label: Section 4.2.637b
sections.original: Quid dixisti ?
sections.translation: Told her what ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.637c
sections.original: Nescio ,
sections.translation: I don ’ t know ; ask her own self .

sections.label: Section 4.2.638b
sections.original: Quid hoc est , uxor ? quidnam hic narravit tibi ?
sections.translation: What ’ s this , wife ? Pray , what has he been telling you ? What is it ? Why are you silent ? Why don ’ t you say what it is ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.639b
sections.original: Quasi tu nescias .
sections.translation: As though you didn ’ t know .

sections.label: Section 4.2.640
sections.original: Me rogas ?
sections.translation: Do you ask me ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.640b
sections.original: Pol haud rogem te , si sciam .
sections.translation: Faith , I shouldn ’ t ask you if I knew .

sections.label: Section 4.2.640c
sections.original: O hominem malum ,
sections.translation: O the wicked fellow ; how he does dissemble . You cannot conceal it ; she knows the matter thoroughly ; by my faith , I ’ ve disclosed everything .

sections.label: Section 4.2.642b
sections.original: Quid id est ?
sections.translation: What is it ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.642c
sections.original: Quando nil pudet
sections.translation: Inasmuch as you are not at all ashamed , and don ’ t wish to confess of your own accord , listen , and attend to this ; I ’ ll both let you know why I ’ m sorrowful , and what he has told me .

sections.label: Section 4.2.645
sections.original: palla mi est domo surrupta .
sections.translation: My mantle has been purloined from me at home .

sections.label: Section 4.2.645b
sections.original: Palla surruptast mihi ?
sections.translation: Mantle purloined from me ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.646
sections.original: Viden ut te scelestus captat ? huic surruptast , non tibi .
sections.translation: D ’ you see how the rogue is catching you up ? ( To MENAECHMUS .) It was purloined from her , not from you ; for certainly if it had been purloined from you , it would now be safe .

sections.label: Section 4.2.648
sections.original: Nil mihi tecum est . sed tu quid ais ?
sections.translation: I ’ ve nothing to do with you . But ( to his WIFE ) what is it you say ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.648b
sections.original: Palla , inquam , periit domo .
sections.translation: A mantle , I say , has been lost from home .

sections.label: Section 4.2.649
sections.original: Quis eam surrupuit ?
sections.translation: Who has stolen it ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.649b
sections.original: Pol istuc ille scit qui illam abstulit .
sections.translation: I faith , he knows that , who took it away .

sections.label: Section 4.2.650
sections.original: Quis is homo est ?
sections.translation: What person was it ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.650b
sections.original: Menaechmus quidam .
sections.translation: A certain Menaechmus .

sections.label: Section 4.2.650c
sections.original: Edepol factum nequiter .
sections.translation: By my troth , ’ twas villanously done . Who is this Menaechmus ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.651b
sections.original: Tu istic , inquam .
sections.translation: You are he , I say .

sections.label: Section 4.2.651c
sections.original: Egone ?
sections.translation: I ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.651d
sections.original: Tu .
sections.translation: You .

sections.label: Section 4.2.651e
sections.original: Quis arguit ?
sections.translation: Who accuses me ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.652
sections.original: Egomet .
sections.translation: I , myself .

sections.label: Section 4.2.652b
sections.original: Et ego . atque huic amicae detulisti Erotio .
sections.translation: I , too ; and you carried it off to Erotium here , your mistress .

sections.label: Section 4.2.653
sections.original: Egon dedi ?
sections.translation: I , gave it her ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.653b
sections.original: Tu , tu istic , inquam . vin adferri noctuam ,
sections.translation: You , you , I say . Do you wish for an owl [*] to be brought here , to say you , you , continually to you ? For we are now quite tired of it .

sections.label: Section 4.2.655
sections.original: Per Iovem deosque omnis adiuro , uxor ( satin hoc est tibi ?)
sections.translation: By Jupiter and all the Gods , I swear , wife ( and isn ’ t that enough for you ?), that I did not give it .

sections.label: Section 4.2.656b
sections.original: Immo hercle vero , nos non falsum dicere .
sections.translation: Aye , and I , by all the powers , that we are telling no untruth .

sections.label: Section 4.2.657
sections.original: Sed ego illam non condonavi , sed sic utendam dedi .
sections.translation: But I haven ’ t given it away , but just only lent it to be made use of .

sections.label: Section 4.2.658
sections.original: Equidem ecastor tuam nec chlamydem do foras nec pallium
sections.translation: But , i ’ faith , for my part , I don ’ t lend either your scarf or your cloak out of the house , to any one , to be made use of . ’ Tis fair that the woman should lend out of the house the woman ’ s apparel ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.660
sections.original: dare foras , virum virile . quin refers pallam domum ?
sections.translation: the man the man ’ s . But why don ’ t you bring the mantle home again ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.661
sections.original: Ego faxo referetur .
sections.translation: I ’ ll have it brought back .

sections.label: Section 4.2.661b
sections.original: Ex re tua , ut opinor , feceris ;
sections.translation: For your own interest you ’ ll do so , as I think ; for you shall never enter the house to-day unless you bring the mantle with you . I ’ m going home .

sections.label: Section 4.2.663b
sections.original: Quid mihi futurum est , qui tibi hanc operam dedi ?
sections.translation: What ’ s there to be for me , who have given you this assistance ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.664
sections.original: Opera reddetur , quando quid tibi erit surruptum domo .—
sections.translation: Your assistance shall be repaid , when anything shall be purloined from your house .

sections.label: Section 4.2.665
sections.original: Id quidem edepol numquam erit , nam nihil est quod perdam domi .
sections.translation: Then , by my troth , that really will never be ; for nothing have I at home to lose . May the Gods confound you , both husband and wife . I ’ ll make haste to the Forum , for I see clearly that I ’ ve quite fallen out with this family .

sections.label: Section 4.2.668
sections.original: Male mi uxor sese fecisse censet , quom exclusit foras ;
sections.translation: My wife thinks that she does me an injury when she shuts me out of doors ; as though I hadn ’ t another better place to be admitted into .

sections.label: Section 4.2.670
sections.original: si tibi displiceo , patiundum : at placuero huic Erotio ,
sections.translation: If I displease you , I must endure it ; I shall please Erotium here , who won ’ t be shutting me out of her house , but will be shutting me up in her house rather . Now I ’ ll go ; I ’ ll beg her to give me back the mantle that I gave her a while since . I ’ ll purchase another for her — a better one . Hallo ! is any one the porter here ? ( Knocks at EROTIUM ’ S door .) Open here , and some one of you call Erotium before the door .

sections.label: Section 4.3.675
sections.original: Quis hic me quaerit ?
sections.translation: Who ’ s enquiring for me here ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.675b
sections.original: Sibi inimicus magis quam aétati tuae .
sections.translation: One that ’ s more of an enemy to his own self than to yourself [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.3.676
sections.original: Mi Menaechme , cur ante aedis astas ? sequere intro .
sections.translation: My dear Menaechmus ? Why are you standing before the house ? Do follow me in-doors .

sections.label: Section 4.3.676b
sections.original: Mane .
sections.translation: Stop . Do you know why it is that I ’ m come to you ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.677b
sections.original: Scio , ut tibi ex me sit volup .
sections.translation: I know well ; that you may amuse yourself with me .

sections.label: Section 4.3.678
sections.original: Immo edepol pallam illam , amabo te , quam tibi dudum dedi ,
sections.translation: Why no , troth , that mantle which I gave you a while since , give it me back , I entreat you ; my wife has become acquainted with all the transaction , in its order , just as it happened .

sections.label: Section 4.3.680
sections.original: ego tibi redimam bis tanto pluris pallam , quam voles .
sections.translation: I ’ ll procure for you a mantle of twofold greater value than you shall wish .

sections.label: Section 4.3.681
sections.original: Tibi dedi equidem illam , ad phrygionem út ferres , paulo prius ,
sections.translation: Why , I gave it your own self a little while since , that you might take it to the embroiderer ’ s , and that bracelet , too , that you might take it to the goldsmith ’ s that it might be made anew .

sections.label: Section 4.3.683
sections.original: Mihi tu ut dederis pallam et spinter ? numquam factum reperies .
sections.translation: You , gave me the mantle and the bracelet ? You ’ ll find ’ twas never done . For , indeed , after I gave it you a while ago , and went away to the Forum ,

sections.label: Section 4.3.685
sections.original: nunc redeo , nunc te postillac video .
sections.translation: I ’ m but just returning , and now see you for the first time since .

sections.label: Section 4.3.685b
sections.original: Video quam rem agis .
sections.translation: I see what plan you are upon ; that you may defraud me of what I entrusted to you , at that thing you are aiming —

sections.label: Section 4.3.687
sections.original: Neque edepol te defrudandi causa posco ( quin tibi
sections.translation: On my word , I do not ask it for the sake of defrauding you . But I tell you that my wife has discovered the matter .

sections.label: Section 4.3.688b
sections.original: Nec te ultro oravi ut dares :
sections.translation: Nor did I of my own accord beg you to give it me ; of your own accord you yourself brought it me . You gave it me as a present ;

sections.label: Section 4.3.690
sections.original: eandem nunc reposcis : patiar . tibi habe , aufer , utere
sections.translation: now you ’ re asking for the same thing back again . I ’ ll put up with it ; keep it to yourself ; take it away ; make use of it , either yourself or your wife , or squeeze it into your money-box [*] even . After this day , that you mayn ’ t be deceived , you shan ’ t set your foot in this house , since you hold me in contempt , who deserve so well of you . Unless you bring money , you ’ ll be disappointed ; you can ’ t cajole me .

sections.label: Section 4.3.695
sections.original: aliam posthac invenito quám habeas frustratui .—
sections.translation: Find some other woman , henceforth , for you to be disappointing .

sections.label: Section 4.3.696
sections.original: Nimis iracunde hercle tandem . heús tu , tibi dico , mane ,
sections.translation: By my troth , very angry at last . Hallo ! you ; stay , I bid you . Come you back . Will you stay now ? Will you even for my sake come back ? ( EROTIUM goes into her house , and shuts the door .) She has gone indoors , and shut the house . Now I ’ m regularly barred out ; I have neither any credit at home now , nor with my mistress .

sections.label: Section 4.3.700
sections.original: ibo et consulam hanc rem amicos , quid faciendum censeant .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll go and consult my friends on this matter , as to what they think should be done .

sections.label: Section 5.1.701
sections.original: Nimis stúlte dudum feci , quom marsuppium
sections.translation: I did very foolishly a while since , in entrusting my purse to Messenio with the money . I suspect he has got himself into some bad house [*] or other . ( Enter the WIFE of Menaechmus of Epidamnus , from the house .)
